rules.title: Win32 App Detection Script Failure
rules.description: Detects when a Win32 app detection script fails or returns unexpected results.
rules.explanation: A Win32 app detection script failed or did not detect the app after installation. This causes the app to appear as 'not installed' despite successful installation.

remediation.title: Fix detection rules
steps: Review the app's detection rules in Intune
Verify file/registry paths are correct
Test detection script manually on a device

rules.title: App Installation Error
rules.description: Detects app installation failures with specific exit codes or HRESULT error codes and suggests fixes.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed with a specific error code. Common MSI exit codes: 1603 (Fatal error during installation), 1618 (Another installation already in progress), 1619 (Package could not be opened), 1625 (Installation prohibited by policy). Common HRESULT codes are also captured.

remediation.title: Investigate the error code
steps: Check the error code in the rule finding details
Look up the specific error code in Microsoft documentation
Review the IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Verify all prerequisites for the app are met
Check if another installation was running concurrently (error 1618)

rules.title: App Dependency Chain Failure
rules.description: Detects when an app fails because a dependency app failed to install.
rules.explanation: An app installation failed because one of its dependency apps failed to install. Fix the dependency first, then retry.

remediation.title: Fix dependency chain
steps: Identify which dependency app failed
Fix the dependency app first
Verify dependency chain order in Intune

rules.title: Insufficient Disk Space
rules.description: Detects when disk space is critically low during enrollment, which can cause app installation failures.
rules.explanation: Performance monitoring shows disk free space dropped below 5 GB during enrollment. This can cause app installation failures, especially for large Win32 apps.

remediation.title: Free disk space
steps: Check device disk size meets minimum requirements
Clean up temporary files and IME cache (C:\Windows\IMECache)
Consider using a larger disk image

rules.title: Content Download Timeout
rules.description: Detects when app content download times out or fails.
rules.explanation: App content download timed out or failed. This is usually caused by network issues, proxy interference, or CDN problems. The IME log shows a Delivery Optimization timeout or download content error.

remediation.title: Improve download speed
steps: Check network bandwidth
Verify proxy doesn't block *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com
Consider Delivery Optimization configuration

rules.title: Reboot Required Loop
rules.description: Detects when the same app installation is attempted 3 or more times, indicating a reboot-required loop.
rules.explanation: The same app has been installed 3 or more times during this enrollment. This typically indicates the app requires a reboot between install attempts, but the ESP is not honoring the reboot request, causing an install-detect-retry loop.

remediation.title: Fix reboot handling
steps: Check if the app's return code mapping includes reboot codes (3010, 1641)
Verify ESP reboot behavior settings
Consider setting the app to 'Hard reboot' return code in Intune

rules.title: Multiple App Installation Failures
rules.description: Detects when the app tracking summary reports multiple failed app installations.
rules.explanation: The app tracking summary reports 2 or more failed app installations during enrollment. This suggests a systemic issue rather than an isolated app failure.

remediation.title: Review failed apps
steps: Check the individual app_install_failed events for specific error patterns
Look for a common pattern (all MSI apps, all large apps, all from a specific publisher)
Consider whether a shared prerequisite or dependency is failing

rules.title: Slow App Installation
rules.description: Detects app installations that take longer than 10 minutes.
rules.explanation: At least one app installation took longer than 10 minutes. This can indicate slow content delivery, installation overhead, or endpoint performance bottlenecks.

remediation.title: Analyze slow app behavior
steps: Identify the affected app from matched evidence in the rule result
Check network throughput and proxy impact during app download
Consider moving large/non-critical apps out of ESP blocking scope

rules.title: App Download Stalled
rules.description: Detects when app downloads appear to have stalled, based on failed download progress events.
rules.explanation: Download progress events show a failed download, and at least one app installation also failed. This pattern suggests content delivery issues preventing apps from being downloaded successfully.

remediation.title: Investigate download issues
steps: Check network connectivity and bandwidth during the enrollment
Verify DNS resolution for *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com
Check if a proxy or firewall is blocking content delivery
Review Delivery Optimization settings and peer availability

rules.title: App Reverted to Error After Successful Install
rules.description: Detects when a specific app (matched by appId) was first reported as successfully installed but subsequently reported an IME enforcement error — while enrollment completed successfully. The app may be broken post-enrollment.
rules.explanation:
An app was initially reported as **successfully installed** (`app_install_completed`) but the IME (Intune Management Extension) subsequently reported an **Error** state for the same app via an `IME-ERROR-REPORT` event — while the overall enrollment completed successfully.

This pattern occurs when the IME re-evaluates enforcement state after the initial install report and detects a failure, e.g. `EnforcementState` transitions from `Success` → `Error`. The affected app and its details are captured in the matched evidence.

Common causes:
- A post-install detection check fails (detection rule mismatch)
- The enforcement error code `-2016345060` (0x87D13B9C) indicates the app state diverged from expected
- The app may appear installed in the ESP summary but is in an error state on the device

**The enrollment succeeded, but the affected app requires attention.**

remediation.title: Investigate the app's post-install enforcement state
steps: Note the appId and appName from the rule finding — these identify the exact affected app
Check the Intune portal: Devices > [Device] > Apps — confirm the current enforcement state of the app
Review IME logs on the device: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\IntuneManagementExtension\Logs\IntuneManagementExtension.log
Search the log for the appId and look for 'EnforcementState' transitions (Success → Error) near the enrollment time
Error -2016345060 (0x87D13B9C) typically indicates a detection failure after install — verify the app's detection rule in Intune
Trigger a manual sync (Company Portal or Settings > Accounts > Access work or school > Sync) to force re-evaluation

remediation.title: Fix detection or installation issues
steps: If detection fails: correct the detection rule (file path, registry key, or PowerShell script) in the Intune app configuration
If the app itself is broken: redeploy or reassign the app to trigger a fresh install
Consider adding a post-install validation script to catch this scenario proactively

rules.title: Slow Network Causing App Installation Failure
rules.description: Correlates slow download speeds with app installation failures while disk space is adequate, confirming network as root cause (not disk).
rules.explanation:
App installation failed while the device has sufficient disk space (>10 GB free). Download progress events indicate network-related issues are the likely root cause.

This rules out disk space as a factor and points to network bandwidth, proxy throttling, or CDN connectivity issues.

remediation.title: Investigate network throughput
steps: Check bandwidth to *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com (CDN)
Verify proxy is not throttling large downloads
Consider enabling Delivery Optimization for peer-to-peer caching

remediation.title: Reduce download load
steps: Stagger app deployments to reduce concurrent downloads
Pre-cache large apps using Delivery Optimization or ConfigMgr
Increase ESP timeout if downloads are slow but completing

rules.title: Disk Space Exhaustion Caused Installation Failure
rules.description: Correlates declining disk free space with app installation failure, confirming insufficient storage as root cause.
rules.explanation:
**Root cause confirmed:** Performance monitoring shows disk free space dropped below 5 GB during enrollment, and apps failed to install. The device likely ran out of storage during app installation.

This is common when deploying many large Win32 apps to devices with small drives (64 GB or 128 GB).

remediation.title: Reduce disk consumption during enrollment
steps: Review total size of all apps deployed during ESP
Move non-critical apps to post-ESP deployment
Use MSIX or Store apps instead of Win32 where possible (smaller footprint)
Clean up IME cache: C:\Windows\IMECache

remediation.title: Use devices with larger drives
steps: Minimum 128 GB recommended for standard deployments
256 GB recommended when deploying large app suites (Office, Visual Studio, etc.)

rules.title: Proxy Configuration Causing Download Failure
rules.description: Correlates an active proxy configuration with app download failures, suggesting the proxy is blocking or throttling Intune content delivery.
rules.explanation:
A proxy (explicit or PAC-based) is configured on this device and app installations failed. Proxies can block or throttle downloads from *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com and other Intune CDN endpoints.

This is a common root cause when apps fail consistently on proxy-connected corporate networks.

remediation.title: Verify proxy bypass for Intune endpoints
steps: Add *.delivery.mp.microsoft.com to proxy bypass list
Add *.do.dsp.mp.microsoft.com (Delivery Optimization) to bypass list
Add login.microsoftonline.com and *.manage.microsoft.com to bypass list
Verify the PAC file is reachable at enrollment time (before user login)

remediation.title: Check proxy authentication
steps: Ensure the proxy does not require user authentication during OOBE/device phase
Consider using machine certificate-based proxy authentication
Test with proxy temporarily bypassed to confirm it is the root cause

rules.title: Windows Hello Provisioning Timeout
rules.description: Detects when Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected timeframe after ESP completion.
rules.explanation: Windows Hello for Business provisioning did not start within the expected time after ESP exit. This may indicate that the Hello policy is not configured, the device doesn't meet prerequisites, or there's a connectivity issue with the Key Registration Service.

remediation.title: Verify Windows Hello configuration
steps: Check if Windows Hello for Business is configured in the tenant
Verify the policy targets the device/user correctly
Ensure the device has a TPM 2.0 (required for WHfB)
Check connectivity to the Key Registration Service (enterpriseregistration.windows.net)

rules.title: High Memory Usage During Enrollment
rules.description: Detects when memory usage exceeds 90% during enrollment, which can cause app installation failures or system instability.
rules.explanation: Memory usage exceeded 90% during enrollment. High memory pressure can cause app installation failures, system slowdowns, and ESP timeouts.

remediation.title: Reduce memory pressure
steps: Review which apps are being installed concurrently during ESP
Consider staggering resource-heavy installations
Check minimum RAM requirements for the device (8 GB recommended)
Investigate if background services are consuming excessive memory

rules.title: Unsupported Windows 11 Version
rules.description: Detects devices running Windows 11 version 22H2 or older, which is no longer supported by Microsoft.
rules.explanation: This device is running Windows 11 version 22H2 (Build 22621) or older, which has reached end of support. Microsoft no longer provides security updates or bug fixes for this version. Devices running unsupported OS versions are exposed to known vulnerabilities and may fail compliance checks.

remediation.title: Update to a supported Windows 11 version
steps: Verify the device meets hardware requirements for Windows 11 23H2 or 24H2
Update the Windows Autopilot deployment profile to use a supported OS image (23H2 or later)
If using a custom OS image, rebuild it with a supported Windows 11 version
For existing devices, deploy a Windows 11 feature update via Intune or Windows Update for Business

rules.title: Enrollment Failed
rules.description: Detects when the Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed.
rules.explanation: The Autopilot enrollment has explicitly failed. Check the enrollment_failed event details and other rule results for the root cause.

remediation.title: Investigate enrollment failure
steps: Check the 'reason' field in the enrollment_failed event for specific failure details
Review other analyze rule results for correlated issues (disk space, network, app failures)
Check the device event timeline for the sequence of events leading to failure
Verify the Autopilot profile and ESP configuration in Intune

rules.title: ESP Blocking App Timeout
rules.description: Detects when the DeviceSetup phase of the ESP took longer than 30 minutes, indicating a stuck or very slow blocking app.
rules.explanation: The ESP DeviceSetup phase took more than 30 minutes. This indicates a blocking app is stuck installing, downloading very slowly, or has entered a retry loop.

remediation.title: Investigate blocking app
steps: Identify which app is blocking (check ESP events)
Verify app content is downloadable
Consider reducing the number of blocking apps

rules.title: Enrollment Duration Exceeded 60 Minutes
rules.description: Detects when the total Autopilot enrollment takes longer than 60 minutes.
rules.explanation: The Autopilot enrollment exceeded 60 minutes. This is unusually long and may indicate stuck apps, slow network, or policy conflicts. The default ESP timeout is 60 minutes, after which the enrollment may be marked as failed.

remediation.title: Investigate long enrollment
steps: Check the app installation timeline - identify which app took the longest
Review download speeds in the performance data
Consider reducing the number of ESP-blocking apps
Check if the ESP timeout policy is configured (default: 60 min)

rules.title: No Machine Certificates Found
rules.description: Detects when no certificates are present in the local machine personal store (LocalMachine\My). Missing certificates may indicate that MDM enrollment certificate provisioning or SCEP certificate deployment failed.
rules.explanation: The expected certificate with the configured subject was not found in the local machine certificate store (LocalMachine\My). Customize the 'value' field in the 'cert_subject_missing' condition to match your expected certificate subject (e.g. 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). The check is case-insensitive (substring match). If the certificate should have been provisioned via SCEP or MDM enrollment, this may indicate a deployment failure.

remediation.title: Verify the expected certificate
steps: Customize the analyze rule: change 'CN=YOUR-CERTIFICATE-SUBJECT' to your expected certificate subject (case-insensitive substring match)
Example subjects: 'CN=Contoso SCEP Cert', 'CN=Contoso Root CA', 'CN=MDM Device Certificate'
Check certlm.msc (Certificates - Local Computer > Personal > Certificates) manually to see what's present

remediation.title: Troubleshoot missing certificate
steps: Check if the device is properly enrolled in Intune (dsregcmd /status)
Verify SCEP/PKCS certificate profiles are assigned and targeted correctly
Check the NDES/SCEP connector status and reachability
Review IntuneManagementExtension logs for certificate enrollment errors
Ensure prerequisite certificates (Trusted Root CA) are deployed before the end-entity profile

templateVariables.name: cert_subject
templateVariables.label: Certificate Subject
templateVariables.description: The expected certificate subject in your environment (e.g., 'CN=Contoso Root CA'). Case-insensitive substring match against the local machine certificate store.

rules.title: Unexpected Local Admin Accounts Detected
rules.description: Detects when the LocalAdminAnalyzer flags unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on the device. This is a known Autopilot bypass technique where admin accounts are created before or during enrollment to gain persistent local admin access.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation:
The agent detected unexpected local user accounts or profile directories on this device that are not in the built-in or tenant-configured allowed list.

This is a security-relevant finding. Creating local admin accounts before or during Autopilot enrollment is a known bypass technique (e.g. via BypassNRO or Shift+F10 during OOBE) that grants persistent local administrator access.

Click the event link below to jump to the local_admin_analysis event and check: unexpected_accounts (local user accounts not on the allowed list), unexpected_profiles (C:\Users profile directories not on the allowed list), bypass_nro (whether the BypassNRO registry flag was set), and confidence_score (the agent's confidence in the finding, 0-100).

If these accounts are expected for your environment (e.g. a service account or helpdesk account), add them to the allowed list in Tenant Settings.

remediation.title: Review the unexpected accounts
steps: Open the session timeline and locate the local_admin_analysis event
Check the 'unexpected_accounts' and 'unexpected_profiles' lists in the event data
Verify whether these accounts are legitimate for your environment

remediation.title: Add expected accounts to the allowed list (false positive)
steps: Go to Settings → Agent Analyzers section
Under 'Allowed Admin Accounts', add the account names that are expected in your environment
Save the settings — future enrollments will no longer flag these accounts

remediation.title: Investigate a potential security issue
steps: Check if the BypassNRO registry flag was set (indicates OOBE was bypassed)
Review the enrollment timeline for Shift+F10 or other OOBE escape indicators
Check whether the unexpected account has admin group membership
Consider re-imaging the device if unauthorized admin access is confirmed

rules.title: Critical Vulnerability Detected
rules.description: Fires when the vulnerability correlation finds critical-severity CVEs or CISA KEV entries in installed software. This indicates the device has software with known, actively exploited vulnerabilities.
rules.author: Autopilot Monitor
rules.explanation: The device has installed software with critical vulnerabilities. Software with CVEs rated CRITICAL (CVSS >= 9.0) or listed in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog was detected. These vulnerabilities are actively targeted by attackers and should be remediated immediately.

remediation.title: Review vulnerability findings
steps: Open the Vulnerability Report section in the session detail
Identify all software with critical or KEV-flagged vulnerabilities
Check if newer versions are available that address these CVEs
Update the affected software in your Intune app deployment

remediation.title: Update deployment packages
steps: Check Intune for outdated app package versions
Update the deployment package to the latest patched version
Re-deploy to affected devices

rules.title: Secure Boot UEFI CA 2023 certificate not deployed
rules.description: Detects devices where the Windows UEFI CA 2023 certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB. These devices will lose Secure Boot security update support when the 2011 certificates expire in June 2026.
rules.explanation:
This device has Secure Boot enabled but the **Windows UEFI CA 2023** certificate has not been deployed to the Secure Boot DB yet.

The original Windows UEFI CA 2011 certificates expire on **June 27, 2026**. After expiration, devices without the 2023 certificate will no longer receive Secure Boot security updates and may fail to boot with updated boot managers.

Current UEFI CA 2023 status: `{{data.uefiCA2023Status}}`

remediation.title: Deploy UEFI CA 2023 certificate
steps: Deploy KB5036210 via Windows Update or WSUS
Use Intune policy to enable Secure Boot DB update (Windows UEFI CA 2023)
Alternatively, use the WinCS CLI tool from Microsoft for manual deployment
Verify deployment: check registry HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecureBoot\Servicing\UEFICA2023Status = 'Updated'
After DB update: ensure the boot manager is updated to a 2023-signed version via monthly cumulative update

relatedDocs.title: Microsoft: Secure Boot certificates expire June 2026
